behavior_summary: Purely combinational qualified binary-to-one-hot decoder. If in_valid is 1 and in is an integer from 0 through 14, out is a 15-bit vector with exactly one bit set high at index equal to the unsigned value of in, i.e. out = 15'b1 << in. If in_valid is 0, out is forced to 15'b0 regardless of in. The clk and rst ports exist only for internal simulation assertions and are not allowed to influence out.
edge_cases: If in_valid is 0, out must be 15'b000000000000000 for all values of in, including 15.
If in_valid is 1 and in is in the valid range 0..14, exactly one output bit must be 1 and all others must be 0.
If in_valid is 1 and in equals 4'b1111 (decimal 15), behavior is explicitly undefined and should not be asserted strictly in the testbench.
There is no sequential state, no latency, and no dependence on clk or rst for functional behavior.
No overflow or wrap-around is specified or allowed for valid inputs; input 15 is not a wrap to bit 0 and is simply undefined.

The output should respond combinationally to changes on in_valid and in; exact propagation delay is not specified, so testbench should sample after a delta cycle or small delay rather than asserting zero-delay timing.
If in contains X or Z values, externally visible behavior is not specified by the natural-language spec and should not be checked strictly.
state_transitions: No internal states or state transitions; module is purely combinational.
priorities: in_valid gating has top functional priority: when in_valid=0, out must be all zeros regardless of in.
clk and rst have no functional priority because they must not affect the combinational decode behavior.
There are no simultaneous control conflicts beyond in_valid qualifying the decode.
mutation_risks: Incorrect output width handling, such as generating 16 bits or misindexing the 15-bit vector.
Off-by-one decode bug, e.g. asserting out[in+1] or out[in-1] instead of out[in].
Bit-order reversal, such as treating input value 0 as MSB out[14] instead of LSB out[0].
Forgetting to gate with in_valid, causing decode activity when in_valid=0.
Driving multiple bits high instead of exactly one bit for valid inputs.
Incorrect handling of boundary inputs 0 and 14.
Improperly defining behavior for input 15, such as wrapping, saturating, or asserting a valid output bit.
Accidentally using clk or rst in the functional logic, creating non-combinational or reset-dependent behavior.
One or more output bits stuck at 0 or 1 due to decoder implementation mistakes.
Partial decode errors affecting only certain input patterns, especially around transitions where multiple input bits differ.
